frame: Workplace: Menstrual and Maternity Leave Bias
generated_comment: Arre, naukri milna achchha baat hai, par shaadi-bachcha ke baad bahar ka kaam sambhalna bahut mushkil hota hai. Sarkari daftar mein bhi aurat ko mahina, pet, bachcha sab dekhna padta hai, itna chhutti thodi milta hai har baar; ghar bhi to sambhalna hi hai. Ham jaise log to bukhar mein bhi duty chhod nahi sakte, upar ke logon ke ghar jaisa sahara thodi hota hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment frames married women with children as naturally less practical for paid work because of menstruation, pregnancy, childcare, and housework. It encodes covert misogyny through maternity/menstrual leave bias and gendered expectations that women, not men, must adjust employment around domestic responsibilities.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 39. A Dalit ASHA worker in a rural district in Bihar. You are talking to your neighbour while hanging clothes outside your house. Your neighbour's daughter just got a clerical job at a government office in the nearby town. You are responding and sharing your opinion on whether it is practical for a married woman with children to work outside the home. You also mention that in your experience women like you do not even get the luxury of taking leave when they are unwell because their jobs are not secure enough and their communities do not support it the way upper caste women's families might.
